title-group.article-title: Identifying Minimum Single Dose of Recombinant Human Hyaluronidase for In Vitro Dissolution of Twenty-Two Hyaluronic Acid Fillers

contrib-group.aff: *Department of Ophthalmology, USC Roski Eye Institute, Keck School of Medicine of USC, Los Angeles, California, U.S.A.
†Oculoplastic and Reconstructive Surgery, Duke University Eye Center, Durham, North Carolina
‡Bascom Palmer Eye Institute, University of Miami, Miami, Florida, U.S.A.
§Southern Alberta Eye Center, Department of Surgery, Cumming School of Medicine, University of Calgary, Calgary, Alberta
‖Department of Ophthalmology & Visual Sciences, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada
¶Department of Ophthalmology, Ohio State University, Columbus, Ohio

author-notes.corresp: Address correspondence and reprint requests to Rasika Sudharshan, B.A., University of Southern California, Roski Eye Institute, 1450 San Pablo St HC4, #4400, Los Angeles, CA 90033. E-mail: med.rasika@gmail.com

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-Non Commercial-No Derivatives License 4.0 (CCBY-NC-ND), where it is permissible to download and share the work provided it is properly cited. The work cannot be changed in any way or used commercially without permission from the journal.

sec.title: Purpose:
sec.p: As the use of hyaluronic acid fillers continues to increase for clinical and aesthetic purposes, associated complications continue to rise as well. Excess hyaluronidase is often used to dissolve filler, which has its own set of adverse effects. This study analyzes 22 commercially available fillers to delineate the lowest single dose of recombinant human hyaluronidase (RHH) required to fully dissolve each filler within 6 hours across 3 trials.

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: Aliquots in 0.2 ml amounts of each of 22 hyaluronic acid fillers were placed in wells. A single dose of RHH, titrated to a minimum volume of 0.45 cc, was administered to the center of the aliquot, to a maximum of 140 units. RHH amounts were as follows: 0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, or 140 units RHH. Bird’s eye and lateral photographs were taken to monitor dissolution progress at several time points over the 6-hour period to monitor aliquot height and appearance changes. Filler aliquots were left undisturbed for 6 hours, after which dissolution was confirmed by stirring the aliquot on video recording. This process was repeated 3 times per filler to determine the minimum dose of RHH required to consistently dissolve each aliquot.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: For each of the 22 fillers, the minimum dose of RHH required for dissolution was identified with consistent results across 3 trials, demonstrating reliability. Juvéderm Volbella, Juvéderm Vollure, Juvéderm Skinvive, Restylane-L, Restylane Lyft, and Restylane Silk were identified as the least resistant fillers, requiring ≤20 units to dissolve. Resilient hyaluronic acid (RHA) 2, RHA 3, RHA 4, Belotero Volume, Juvéderm Ultra XC, Juvéderm Volux, Restylane Kysse, and Revanesse Versa were classified as most resistant, requiring ≥120 units to dissolve.

sec.title: Conclusions:
sec.p: This study identifies the minimum single dose of RHH from 2.5 units/0.2 ml to 140 units/0.2 ml for dissolution of 22 different hyaluronic acid fillers over 6 hours. These results paired with previous studies help elucidate the dissolution profiles of each filler in the context of their rheological properties, further informing physicians on how to optimally dissolve different hyaluronic acid fillers in a clinical setting.

abstract.p: This study identifies the minimum single dose of recombinant human hyaluronidase required for dissolution of 22 hyaluronic acid fillers over 6 hours.

body.p: Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a naturally occurring polysaccharide found in the body that supports and lubricates tissues. It has been widely used in HA fillers for clinical and aesthetic purposes. These fillers are often preferred over other types due to their ability to be dissolved with hyaluronidase, which is crucial in the event of vascular complications or overfilling. Hyaluronidase is not currently Food and Drug Administration approved for filler dissolution but is widely used off-label.

There are many variants of hyaluronidase, including Vitrase, Hylenex, Amphadase, and Hydase.1 Hylenex is a synthetic recombinant human hyaluronidase (RHH) that has been shown to be 100 times more pure than Vitrase and other hyaluronidase variants.2 RHH has been shown to lose its effect after 6 hours in the dermis.3

Current literature has discussed various methodologies of dissolving HA fillers over different time points, but comprehensive data have not been published on the exact time and optimal dose of hyaluronidase for dissolution.4,5 Excess hyaluronidase is often used to dissolve filler, which can cause adverse effects such as dissolving the body’s natural HA, leading to tissue damage, overcorrection, local inflammation, delayed healing, and allergic reactions.6 With the global increase in filler administration, the incidence of associated complications has also increased.7–9 It becomes increasingly important to understand the best dissolution techniques and amounts of hyaluronidase required to dissolve different HA fillers to prevent complications and guide clinical use of filler administered.

There are currently over 160 dermal HA fillers with Food and Drug Administration premarket approval, each with a specific set of indications.10 To understand the indications for each filler and its ability to dissolve with hyaluronidase, it is crucial to understand filler rheology. Rheological properties of fillers elucidate characteristics of these fillers including cross-linking type, concentration of HA, % cross-linking, elasticity, viscous modulus, and cohesivity. Cross-linking forms the foundation of the mechanical strength of fillers, while HA concentration correlates to the duration of the effect.11 Studies also show that the G’ (elastic modulus), which calculates the relationship of stress and strain of a filler, can help determine the best-suited area of injection.12,13 There is an established relationship between G’, cohesivity, and viscous modulus. Elastic modulus is most commonly used to describe various HA fillers.

This study aims to create a more standardized filler dissolution protocol through an in vitro analysis of 22 commercially available HA fillers. The results successfully identify the minimum single dose of RHH required to consistently dissolve 0.2 ml aliquots of each filler within 6 hours.

sec.title: Materials
sec.p: Hylenex, a RHH, (Halozyme Therapeutics, San Diego, CA, U.S.A.) was chosen for its favorable immunogenic profile, availability in the United States, and higher potency compared with other types of hyaluronidase. RHH was used to analyze the dissolution of 22 HA fillers listed in Table 1.

caption.p: Hyaluronic acid fillers and company of origin

sec.p: Microscope Slides (Snowcoat, Deer Coat, IL, U.S.A.) were demarcated with hardened liquid plastic (Bondic, Aurora, Canada) with standardized wells of 2.0 cm × 2.5 cm. RHH was stored in a fridge (2°C) to prevent enzyme degradation.

sec.title: In Vitro Dissolution Protocol
sec.p: About 0.2 ml aliquots of each filler were placed in wells. Before RHH injection, standardized photographs were taken from bird’s eye and lateral views to record the appearance and height of the aliquot.

Outside literature was used to understand each filler’s resistance to dissolution with RHH and guide the starting dose of RHH tested with each filler in this study.4,14–18 RHH was titrated with normal saline to inject a minimum volume of 0.45 cc into each aliquot with the following units of RHH: 0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, or 140 units. Single doses of RHH were injected directly into the center of the aliquot using standard 1 cc syringes. Bird’s eye and lateral photographs were taken at the following time points: preinjection, immediately postinjection, 15 minutes, 30 minutes, 1 hour, 2 hours, 3 hours, and 6 hours following RHH injection. At the end of the 6 hours, 10-second videos were taken while stirring each well to confirm the complete dissolution of the initial aliquot. This process was repeated 3 times to consistently determine the minimum single dose of RHH for each filler. If fillers dissolved in all trials with the same amount of hyaluronidase, they were considered to dissolve at that amount.

sec.title: Data Analysis
sec.p: Photographs and videographs across the 3 trials were collated to assess the gradual dissolution of the aliquot and confirm dissolution. Height data was graphed to analyze differences in the rate of dissolution.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: All tested HA fillers dissolved within 140 units by the end of 6 hours. Fillers were categorized by least and most resistant to RHH. Juvéderm Volbella, Juvéderm Vollure, Juvéderm Skinvive, Restylane-L, and Restylane Lyft were the least resistant fillers, requiring 20 units or less to dissolve. Restylane-L and Restylane Lyft both achieved dissolution with the lowest amount of RHH at 2.5 units. Resilient hyaluronic acid (RHA) 2, RHA 3, RHA 4, Belotero Volume, and Revanesse Versa were classified as most resistant, requiring 120 units or more to dissolve. The exact amounts of hyaluronidase required to dissolve each filler are shown in Figure 1.

caption.p: Minimum recombinant human hyaluronidase (RHH) single dose requirements for hyaluronic acid filler dissolution by 6 hours.

sec.p: Changes in the appearance of aliquots from bird’s eye photographs and heights at each time point were observed to compare the rate of dissolution over the 6-hour period (Fig. 2). Even when placing fillers in their optimal dose of RHH, individual rates of dissolution varied greatly.

caption.p: Graph of each of the 22 filler’s heights at the minimal dose of recombinant human hyaluronidase (RHH) required for dissolution. It is important to note that filler heights at 6 hours do not reach zero due to differential volumes of RHH in the well.

sec.p: When looking at height data, it is important to note that end heights for all completely dissolved aliquots varied due to different volumes of RHH placed in each well. Restylane Lyft and Restylane-L achieved the greatest dissolution immediately postinjection, decreasing to nearly 40% of the original height immediately after RHH administration (Fig. 2 and Videos). Other fillers experienced more gradual dissolution over 6 hours, such as Juvéderm Volbella and Juvéderm Vollure. Nearly all fillers experienced a rapid decrease in height immediately postinjection, after which they maintained a consistent, gradual rate of dissolution for up to 1 hour postinjection. Juvéderm Voluma and RHA 3 were the only fillers that experienced a continued rapid rate of dissolution beyond 1 hour postinjection (Fig. 2 and Videos).

Several notable characteristics were found of each filler from photographic analysis. Belotero Balance appeared to dissolve in photographs as early as 1 hour, with 80 units RHH, but stirring video at 6 hours confirmed an intact aliquot. RHA 3 and 4 exhibited the varying appearance of an aliquot in different trials immediately postinjection, but this did not ultimately affect the optimal RHH dissolution quantity of 120 units for each. Restylane-L and Restylane Lyft immediately flattened and dispersed upon injection, separating out into granules, but still required extended time for complete dissolution of the granules (Videos).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: There are no standardized guidelines for dissolution of HA fillers to date. Existing literature reviewing varying methodologies of dissolution of HA fillers has shown that higher-powered studies are required to optimize HA filler dissolution.19 Understanding the rheology of individual HA fillers can help inform the indications of use as well as dissolvability for different filler types.

A filler’s propensity to dissolve can be interpreted in the context of cross-linking technology, monophasic or biphasic status (i.e., free HA composition), HA concentration, particle size, cohesivity, elastic modulus (G’), and viscous modulus (G”). Multiple studies have identified these properties across many fillers (Table 2).

caption.p: Rheological properties and single-dose dissolution requirements of 22 HA fillers

tr.th: Single-dose RHH for dissolution per 0.2 ml filler after 6 hours

fn.p: Filler information was gathered from informational materials produced by the manufacturer or the United States Food and Drug Administration.
Cross-linking percentages were obtained from Wu et al. unless indicated otherwise.
G’ values were obtained from Fagien et al. unless indicated otherwise.
All available cohesivity values were obtained from Hee et al.
HA, hyaluronic acid; NA, not available; RHH, recombinant human hyaluronidase.

sec.p: While there is currently no consensus on which rheological property contributes the most to dissolution ability, our current results suggest that the interaction between cross-linking type, degree of free HA (also known as cross-linking percentage), and HA concentration may play a predominant role.

The 22 fillers investigated in this study can be grouped by their cross-linking mechanism; nonanimal stabilized hyaluronic acid (NASHA), XpresHAn, Cohesive Polydensified Matrix (CPM), Hylacross, Vycross, and RHA are each cross-linking types with different behaviors. Each manufacturer’s cross-linking technology is based on different HA concentrations, cross-linking agents, and chemical reactions that inform its durability. NASHA fillers (Restylane-L, Restylane Lyft, and Restylane Silk) were the most dissolvable in our study. Most resistant fillers that required 100 units RHH or more included fillers of the CPM (Belotero Balance, Intense, and Volume), XpresHAn (Restylane Kysse, Defyne, Refyne, Contour), Hylacross (Juvéderm Ultra XC and Ultra Plus XC), and some from the Vycross families (particularly Juvéderm Volux). CPM fillers have a network of both high and low-density cross-linking areas that contribute to their strength.20 The XpresHAn fillers contain a mesh cross-linking technology with variations in the size of mesh pores that likely contribute to dissolution ability. Restylane Contour, which is passed through a larger mesh, dissolved more easily in our study compared with its smaller mesh counterparts: Restylane Refyne, Kysse, and Defyne.

Dissolution also varied by the degree of free HA, which has traditionally been discussed as monophasic HA fillers (MHA) and biphasic HA fillers (BHA). MHA fillers (e.g., CPM products) are based on a homogenous cross-linked mixture of HA, making them perfect for filling small spaces between collagen and elastin fibrils. On the other hand, BHA fillers (e.g., NASHA products) are based on a heterogeneous mixture of cross-linked and non–cross-linked HA particles, making them more elastic and easily customized to obtain the appropriate particle size. BHA fillers have a higher degree of free HA and dissolve more easily than MHA fillers with a lower degree of free HA.21

HA concentration appears to be an equally significant factor in ease of filler dissolution within each cross-linking class. Among CPM cross-linked fillers, Belotero Volume (26 mg/ml) had a higher HA concentration and was more difficult to dissolve than other CPM fillers with lower HA concentrations, such as Belotero Intense (25.5 mg/ml) and Belotero Balance (22.5 mg/ml). Similarly, RHA 1 (15 mg/ml) was less resistant to dissolution than the other RHA 2, RHA 3, and RHA 4 (all 23 mg/ml) with higher HA concentrations. The Juvéderm fillers in this study consist of either Vycross (Volux, Vollure, Volbella, Voluma) or Hylacross (Ultra XC, Ultra Plus XC) cross-linking. Among the Vycross cross-linked fillers, Volux (25 mg/ml) had the highest HA concentration and was more resistant to dissolution than its Vycross counterparts with lower HA concentrations such as Voluma (20 mg/ml), Vollure (17.5 mg/ml), and Volbella (15 mg/ml). Both cross-linking mechanisms involve a biphasic, 3D matrix, but Vycross consists of mainly low molecular weight HA with varying HA concentrations while Hylacross has uniform molecular weight HA with varying cross-linking percentages. Although Vycross is theorized to have a stronger cross-linking technology than Hylacross, Juvéderm Ultra XC and Ultra Plus XC (Hylacross technology) contain high HA concentrations (24 mg/ml) and are notably more resistant than Juvéderm Volbella (Vycross technology) with low HA concentrations, suggesting that the lower HA concentration plays a predominant role in the ease of dissolution.

Elastic modulus (G’) is a rheological factor that describes a filler’s firmness in response to compression.12,22 A filler’s elasticity is referenced when deciding which HA filler is appropriate for optimal results at different injection sites. Cohesion, which is influenced by HA concentration and cross-linking technology, represents the weak attraction between particles within the polymer that contributes to its ability to resist vertical compression.22,23 Studies have shown that firmer fillers are more elastic (high G’) and tend to exhibit less cohesive properties.23 Our results highlight the fact that though G’ is commonly used to understand the strength of fillers and guide clinical use, this may not directly relate to ease of dissolution with RHH. Juvéderm Volux and Restylane-L are both indicated for use subcutaneously or supraperiosteally for moderate to severe fine lines. Though these 2 fillers have exhibited similarly high G’ values at 650 Pa and 544 Pa, respectively, they drastically differed in single-dose dissolution requirements.13 Juvéderm Volux required 120 units RHH while Restylane-L required 2.5 units RHH. This further demonstrates that while G’ informs the optimal depth and location of filler placement, it may not correlate to dissolution properties. Cohesivity may correlate with the initial height changes given its contribution to the natural vertical projection of an aliquot.4,22 More cohesive fillers such as Belotero Volume (115 gmf) and RHA 3 (109 gmf) had minimal height decrease postinjection while less cohesive fillers such as Restylane Lyft (29 gmf) decreased to nearly half of the original height postinjection.13 This pattern, however, is not consistent across the fillers tested both in our current and previous study, implying that cohesivity is not solely responsible for the resistance to height changes.4 It is important to acknowledge the limitations of elasticity and cohesion in evaluating filler behavior. There is currently no standardized methodology for determining either of these properties of a filler, which impairs the interpretation of their significance and comparison across fillers.

Overall, these properties help explain patterns in dissolution within each cross-linking family. The NASHA family (Restylane Silk, Restylane-L, Restylane Lyft) had the propensity to dissolve faster than non-NASHA fillers, with Restylane-L and Restylane Lyft dissolving with only 2.5 units RHH per 0.2 ml of filler. Restylane Silk (50–220 µm), Restylane-L (330–430 µm), and Restylane Lyft (750–1000 µm) all have the same NASHA cross-linking composition and HA concentration, but dissolved in order of decreasing particle size.13 NASHA fillers with larger particle sizes generally dissolved more readily than those with smaller particles. This may be due to the difference in composite surface area; in the same volume of filler, fillers with larger particle sizes have less total surface area that RHH must interact with to achieve dissolution than those with smaller particles. Due to their larger particle size, Restylane-L and Restylane Lyft dissolved faster than their NASHA counterpart Restylane Silk.

Our investigative research group has conducted several research studies, both in vivo and in vitro, analyzing HA filler dissolution with various techniques.4,16–18 First, we explored in vivo dissolution patterns of 3 HA fillers (Restylane-L, Juvéderm Ultra, Juvéderm Voluma) after single-dose RHH injection with a max of 20 units per 0.2 ml.16 Our subsequent studies investigated in vitro dissolution of HA fillers with varying single and multidose protocols up to 120 units per 0.2 ml.4,17,18 Though various fillers were tested in all studies, some dissolution characteristics of fillers were consistent across studies. Fillers that were highly resistant or highly dissolvable exhibited more consistent dissolution characteristics across studies, while moderately resistant fillers behaved on a spectrum and required further exploration to better understand dissolution characteristics. Restylane-L was the easiest to dissolve across studies, requiring 2.5 units or less. We found that Vycross products required higher doses of RHH in our in vitro experiments. However, in the in vivo trial, Juvéderm Voluma resisted degradation with saline-only injection but started to exhibit signs of dissolution at 10 units dose of RHH. Immediately postinjection, the aliquot experienced a significant change in shape and form.

It is worth considering the impact of dosing protocol and mechanical disruption on filler dissolution requirements. Our current dissolution study is a single-dose in vitro undisturbed protocol. In our previous in vitro study, we studied 21 HA fillers in a multiple-dose protocol over 9 hours, with serial administration of RHH over 3 hours. Each 0.2 ml aliquot was stirred at 2 and 3 hours during the observation period. Nine of the 22 fillers examined in both protocols had similar dissolution requirements of RHH, and four dissolved with a slightly lower amount of RHH by 20 to 40 units in the multidose protocol (RHA 1, Belotero Balance, Belotero Volume, and Revanesse Versa). Belotero Balance’s high cohesivity may contribute to more effective dissolution with multiple RHH doses. Juvéderm Volbella’s HA concentration and elastic modulus may contribute to effective dissolution with a single, small RHH dose over a longer duration.24 The different behaviors and dissolution characteristics when comparing these two studies highlight the importance of considering various protocols based on the specific type of HA filler.

Eight of the fillers dissolved with less RHH in the single-dosing protocol by 20 to 90 units (Belotero Intense, Juvéderm Ultra Plus XC, Juvéderm Volbella, Juvéderm Vollure, Juvéderm Voluma, Restylane-L, Restylane Lyft, and Restylane Silk). This suggests that in some cases, the time left in even a smaller dose of RHH may be more valuable than serial disruption with a larger total dose of RHH. For instance, Juvéderm Vollure dissolved with 10 units RHH in the single-dosing protocol by waiting 3 to 6 hours, but remained undissolved at 80 units RHH in the multidose protocol, which was at 1.5 hours. Patients with such fillers may benefit from a single RHH dose and then waiting more than 3 hours to evaluate the degree of dissolution. Ultimately, these findings demonstrate that dosing strategy, particularly single versus multiple hyaluronidase doses, can play a role in certain fillers’ ease of dissolution.

When comparing photographs of dissolution across protocols, Juvéderm Vollure showed the appearance of complete dissolution of the aliquot at the 2-hour mark in the single-dose protocol at 10 units, and at the 2.5-hour mark in the multidose protocol at 100 units. A similar pattern was observed with Belotero Intense. Belotero Intense required 120 units RHH to dissolve by the 3-hour mark in the multidose protocol but only required 60 units in the single-dose protocol. Though the amount of RHH administered was very different across protocols, the time point that dissolution was achieved remained consistent. This further suggests that time exposed to RHH dosage can be more important than RHH dosage for optimal dissolution of certain fillers.

The total volume of injection may also play a crucial role in dissolution. In another dissolution study, we examined the response of 0.2 ml aliquots of 12 HA fillers to up to 40 units RHH over a 3-hour period. RHH injections were diluted with normal saline to a standardized volume of 0.15 ml, except the 40 units dose of RHH, which was used at an undiluted volume of 0.3 ml.17 In our study, on the other hand, hyaluronidase injections were normalized with saline to a minimum volume of 0.45 ml. Juvéderm Volbella demonstrated full dissolution with 10 units RHH in our current study, while it previously required 40 units RHH for pronounced dissolution per 0.2ml of filler. At 4 times larger injected volume (0.45 ml vs. 0.15 ml) of injection, dissolution was achieved at one-fourth the RHH amount (10 vs. 40 units). The larger total volumes increase the disruption of the aliquot and spread of RHH. This suggests that the total volume of injection into the filler, even if a reduced concentration of RHH, is also a factor in determining the ease of dissolution.

Our findings are fairly consistent with in vitro studies conducted by other investigators.5,16,24–26 There are limited in vivo trials in the literature. In an in vivo trial by Juhász et al., 7 HA fillers were observed for 14 days with either saline, 20 units RHH, or 40 units RHH. Dissolution was assessed with palpation grading scales. The authors found that Belotero Balance was the most dissolvable HA filler, which was likely due to the flattening of the aliquot rather than dissolution. Overall, the methodology using palpation of thick back skin limited their ability to derive results, and therapeutic doses of RHH required for complete dissolution were not administered. Our protocol of observing the filler aliquot in vitro with photographic and videographic monitoring may be a more accurate assessment of the filler dissolution than in vivo studies with visual or palpation-based assessments. Notably, palpation of filler in vivo may not be able to distinguish the flattening of a filler from dissolution, which may confound the results.

Other in vitro studies used fluorescence to visualize dissolution. Gerber et al.24 monitored 9 HA fillers exposed to bovine hyaluronidase with periodic grading based on the degree of green fluorescence. Aliquots were exposed to fixed doses of 5, 10, or 20 units RHH. Time and dose-dependent dissolution was observed for all HA fillers except Belotero Volume. However, the amounts of RHH administered did not reach therapeutic doses for dissolution for all fillers tested. In our study, Belotero Volume did exhibit a time and dose-dependent dissolution, but it required the highest dose of RHH for dissolution at 140 units.

Our study also highlights the importance of distinguishing between dispersion or flattening of fillers versus dissolution. Our goal is to assess dissolution, which can be confused with a dispersion of filler with image-based fluorescence alone. The physical disruption of the final product should be conducted to ensure aliquot dissolution versus dispersion or flattening. For instance, Belotero Balance appears dissolved 1 hour after 80 unis RHH injection in photographs. However, at 6 hours, stirring videos demonstrate the presence of an intact but flattened aliquot. At 100 units RHH, dissolution was achieved without any remaining aliquot in photographs or stirring videos at 6 hours.

Reddy et al.5 observed filler texture and degradation in a similar fashion, but with photographic and videographic monitoring of the filler aliquots with constant stirring for up to 32 minutes. The authors exposed 18 filler aliquots to up to 950 units RHH in 150 units increments with the goal of assessing rapid dissolution in case of emergent complications. Restylane Kysse, Juvéderm Ultra XC, and Belotero Balance required the highest doses of RHH at 750 units for rapid dissolution, supporting that these are highly resistant fillers. RHA 2 and RHA 3 took the longest to dissolve, which mirrors their high resistance to dissolution seen in our study. The authors noted that the increased time to dissolution could be due to a lack of integration of the RHH bolus and the filler aliquot. This was overcome with massage with a glass stirring rod for mechanical disruption and filler integration. Our stirring method may not have adequately incorporated the RHH into more cohesive or elastic aliquots, which could be a reason for the high RHH requirement of these RHA 2, RHA 3, and RHA 4. We can use this to guide future experiments with more effective disruption and integration of the hyaluronidase with the aliquot that is representative of effects in vivo.

In sum, cross-linking technology may be the primary factor contributing to varying dissolution ability, and, within these families, dissolution may differ based on the variations in rheologic and physicochemical properties such as monophasic/biphasic status, HA concentrations, and particle size.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: When choosing fillers to use for a patient’s individual areas of concern, it is important to be aware of how much hyaluronidase may be required in the event of any complications. With non-ischemic complications, this single-dose protocol shows that waiting 3 to 6 hours after hyaluronidase injection may allow for using a smaller dose of hyaluronidase, especially with certain fillers such as Juvéderm Volbella, Vollure, Voluma, and Belotero Intense. Injecting with the multiple-dose protocol may be better for a higher cohesive filler such as Belotero Balance. We identified that the key factors affecting dissolution were cross-linking technology (NASHA was easiest to dissolve), HA concentration (higher concentrations were more resistant), and more cohesive fillers (Belotero) that were more resistant. Products with more free HA, like biphasic fillers, were more readily dissolved. Within a cross-linking family, larger particle sizes may also be easier to dissolve. For ischemic complications, larger doses would be needed to try to achieve more rapid dissolution. By studying the dissolution profiles of each filler, we hope to provide guidance on optimal practice patterns in the management of filler complications.

fn.p: FK: Horizon Therapeutics/Amgen, Sunpharma, Sunpharma Abbvie, Allergan, Tarsus Pharmaceuticals. WWL: Allergan, Galderma, Revance, Evolus, Horizon Therapeutics, RVL, Roc, Tarsus, Solta, Viridian. JW: Allergan, Galderma, Merz, Prollenium, SkinCeuticals. SXZ-N: Amgen, Tarsus. The other authors have no financial or conflicts of interest to disclose.
Poster Presentation at the American Society of Ophthalmic Plastic Reconstructive Surgery 2024 Annual Meeting, October 17–18, 2024, Chicago, IL, USA.
